item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like a lot of it, there's just a couple things I would change.
In the description, you say it travels until it reaches Sicily, but you don't say what happens after that. Does it then go back out into the ocean? Does it go dormant? What happens?

Also I think the addendums could use a little more subtlety. The first two documents are pretty cool because it lets the reader piece together why this thing was created and what its purpose is, but still leaves it open. But the second two documents just explain exactly what it is, taking all of the mystery away. I still like them, but I would tweak them a little bit.

Finally, I have no idea what this automaton is supposed to look like other than it being "teal". I assumed it would be relatively small and was surprised when I read it could swallow thousands of liters of sea water. A little bit of description of its size and shape would help a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Note that liters are already volume, and do not need to be cubed. Cubic meters might be what you want here, judging by scale.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is great! It definitely provides a lens into the world of Mekhanites and what some people during that time were like. Korinsia is the star here, of course, but the inclusion of Tyre and another Mekhanite city provides excellent world building.

A little critique here is that dedicating an entire MTF to observing this thing is way overkill. I think that a couple level 1 agents could accomplish the same job just as well as an entire MTF.

Tiny nitpicks, but well done! It's a fun little SCP and a good quick read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's something oddly compelling about this. I acknowledge the story being told here and think it's a rather interesting one. The character Korinsia built this automaton to aid her and other Mekhanites like her, but when the Sarkics invaded, she ordered the automaton to flee and never return. And for thousands of years, it's been doing exactly that.

The automaton isn't sentient, so it's not like there's an emotional connection to be drawn here. Although, Korinsia certainly treats it as though it's more than just a mere machine. It's quaint that the automaton "lives on" with evidence of Korinsia and her life, and will continue to do so for as long as its machine parts can operate. Perhaps I find it compelling because of how quaint it is while managing to be more than the sum of its parts.

Anyway, I do like the story being told here. The only thing keeping me from upvoting is some of the awkward phrasing and semi-nonclinical sections. It's a very marginal thing but it's there. I'd love to see more like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed image formatting for you. There's a couple typos I found, but I'm not sure if I should fx them or not:

I have witnessed something terrific.

Either this is a typo and it should be horrific, or it isn't and the letter doesn't sound awed enough.

Magnes refers to the chemical element Magnesia

The element is magnesium. Magnesia is a mineral that contains magnesium oxide. I'm not sure which of the two you mean.

Anyway, I think the best part of this is the implied character of Matriarch Korinsia. Passionate, resentful of the current situation of her people, apparently loving her creations enough she feels the need to not only spare them from war but apologize. But the article overall feels as if it were missing something. I like the anomaly and story you have created, but I don't feel satisfied by the end of it. Novote, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very nice, I enjoyed reading this. I don’t know if it’s just what I like but, ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

+1
